item.title: Today someone said he makes $125k a year and then another person said >what do you do to make $125k >that is a lot >are… — 173 replies — 561.6/hr
item.content:encoded:
Today someone said he makes $125k a year and then another person said >what do you do to make $125k >that is a lot >are…

Open thread • Replies: 173 • Updated: 2026-03-11 17:52 UTC

OP

Today someone said he makes $125k a year and then another person said
>what do you do to make $125k
>that is a lot
>are you in LA?
>why are you lying?
Then I said
>150k is middle class
>no offense, when others don’t believe others, it means they are poor or make less
Then he replied
>150k is not middle class
>60k-80k is middle class
I said
>$125k cannot afford a middle class lifestyle and most jobs paying $125k are in big cities and there is high cost of living
Then he starts lying and saying he is 13
The conversation is just him seething someone makes $125k and arguing that 150k is not middle class.
I tell him due to increased cost of living and wealth gap, even 1m is nothing and 10% of 50% of the consumption and 90% are not doing well.
Why are poor people always seething?
$125k isn’t even middle class. How poor do you guys that guy is? 40k? 70k?
He was really defensive about $150k being middle class.
Do poor people think they are middle class?
This isn’t the first time this has happened.

Early replies (first 3)

61965995
>>61965964
Middle class is self sufficient, it requires an income that can support a household, retirement, children, education(including college), stay at home wife (or) nanny, 2+ modern cars, insurance, 1-3 vacations/yr . I would say 125k-250k depending on area. Lower class normies think they are middle class, middle class is mostly dead. Upper class has excess income after meeting those needs. Lower class requires gov assistance(welfare, healthcare, public school, etc).

61966005
>>61965964
>>$125k cannot afford a middle class lifestyle and most jobs paying $125k are in big cities and there is high cost of living
When people ssay middle class they mean they've got a wife and together they make about 140k or something and can easily afford a suburban house in areas within 2 hours of major cities, especially in southern and midwestern cities like Omaha and Dallas. Your idea of middle class is jetsetting around and banging sloots whenever you want.

61966016
>>61965995
> 1-3 vacations/yr .
1 vacation, maybe dude.
3? Lmao.
I think middle class lifestyle now requires a lot higher income than what people think now.
That wasn’t the first time someone got really defensive with “no, 60-80k is middle class.”
Fucking annoying but good conversation I guess. The ignorance is annoying.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61967337
>>61965964
>>61965995
LOL

61967344
>>61967295
>>61967301
150k is middle class.
Now leave.
/thread.
Everyone else, thanks for the discussion.

61967505
>>61965964
>Why are poor people always seething?
Because we’re broke nigga now hurry up and deflect those boulders, Pee-Lon

61967568
>>61965964
$150k HOUSEHOLD income is middle class. Comfortably.
But middle class in 2025 has a household with two working adults, often a primary income earner making $80k-$90k a year with a partner making $60k-$70k a year.
An individual making $150k a year is wealthy. This isn't really up for debate. The statistics are easy to find.
With $150k a year you can comfortably support a family with a stay at home mom, which in 2025 means you are wealthy. But more likely you are also living in a dual income household where your partner is making anywhere from $70k to $150k (high income earners are more likely to be married to other high income earners), meaning your household income is $220k to $300k.
When your household is bringing in that much it also means you are gonna quickly start accruing income generating assets, meaning overtime your income is only gonna get larger even if your salary remains the same, an obvious sign that you are beyond the typical 'middle class'.
The reality that as a single person on $150k you can only afford what is a typical middle class lifestyle for a dual income household is besides the point. The very fact that you can afford this on a single income means you are considered wealthy. That is just the economic reality today.

61967661
>>61967568
Cool. The average person is so poor.

61967666
>>61967568
> With $150k a year you can comfortably support a family with a stay at home mom
Fuck that shit. I aint letting a girl not work. lmao.

61967673
>>61967666

61967711

61967731
I make $125k before taxes right now. Is that a lot? I always assumed I was a little underpaid since I don't ask for raises or job hop.

61967743
>>61967731
Gross? It's alright. Net? Bretti gud. Equivalent to making 62k twenty years ago. High end of lower middle class.

61967992
>>61965995

COL is the only thing that matters

61968004
>>61967215
If you own a home with no debt and you live in an area with low COL, you can save 25-35% of your income for anything you want. You can easily do this on blue collar income.

Cityniggers are the true poors.

61968115
>>61968004
City people that can afford it are not poor,ruralfag

61968117
>>61968004

61968118
>>61967992
You’re fucking retarded.

61968164
>>61966297
Man it's you back again. You've always said previously you're a NEET and your brother is rich and pays for your life. But it can't be much of a life if you waste it baiting people on biz who weren't born at the finish line.

61968189

61968190

ocpd namenigger thread.

61968224

Things poor people say.

61968227
>>61968164
> who weren't born at the finish line.
Everyone, when you add their parents and grandparents, had 100 years to make. All the high iq people made it

61968231
>>61968202
But the jobs are even rarer and your work load higher

61968235
Bloomberg literally right now said
>middle income are starting to join the lower income in feeling the pain of rising prices.

61968302
Class is not derived from monthly income level. If you think it is, that's a giveaway that you're working class.

Middle class people own their home outright, no debts, and don't have to worry about work. IE if they lose their job, they could support themselves for at least a full year without incurring a hit to their standard of living, or getting into turmoil.

Middle class people can work fun jobs for low income, and still live middle class lives, because they have their home paid off. Higher middle class will own stocks that provide a stable income for themselves as well as their home.

If you have to work for a living, you are working class. Regardless of what your takehome is.

If not going to work means you lose your home, you're definitely not middle class.

61968307
>too stupid to understand cost of living
Sorry for the people conversing with you

sage for off topic shit thread

item.content:encoded:
SECOR Protocol Proposal for Nerva Core Update

Open thread • Replies: 4 • Updated: 2026-03-11 17:35 UTC

OP

>Legacy CPU-only privacy coin from 2018 that refuses to die
Former Monero dev has joined the Nerva project and is working on implementing the SECOR protocol into Nerva to improve block propagation and increase transaction/confirmation speeds. In addition there are numerous bug fixes and privacy improvements planned to be released with the next core update.
https://github.com/nerva-project/nerva/pull/40

I stumbled on this project when it was shilled on biz back in January. Everyone kept calling it a shitcoin but its looking more like a sleeper project that might blow up.

item.title: WEAT - the play everyone is missing — 38 replies — 24.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
WEAT - the play everyone is missing

Open thread • Replies: 38 • Updated: 2026-03-11 16:35 UTC

OP

This is potentially the most consequential second-order effect from the oil crisis and almost nobody in retail trading is talking about it yet.
Oil and natural gas aren't just fuel — they're the foundational feedstock for nitrogen fertilizer.
The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia, which becomes urea, ammonium nitrate, and other fertilizers.
Roughly 2-3% of global energy consumption goes to fertilizer production alone. When natural gas prices doubled after Russia invaded Ukraine, fertilizer prices tripled and we got a global food price crisis.

Now consider: European natural gas has nearly doubled already in one week. Qatar - one of the world's largest LNG exporters - declared force majeure. 20% of global LNG transits Hormuz. Europe rebuilt its entire energy security strategy around LNG after cutting off Russian gas.

The third-order effect: We're entering spring planting season in the Northern Hemisphere
RIGHT NOW. Farmers make input decisions in March-April. If fertilizer prices spike 50-100% (which is beginning), farmers either plant less, use less fertilizer (lower yields), or absorb costs they can't recover. The food price inflation from this shows up in August-October harvests -
5-6 months from now.

They can release strategic oil reserves. There are no strategic fertilizer reserves. Food prices are about to be fucked, and as you can see from the chart - if you get in early, you can profit. Let retards from Reddit chase oil prices around. Go ahead and wait for them at the finish line.

You’re welcome fuckfaces

Nitrogen fertilizer is synthesized from natural gas, not mined. 33% of global fertilizer trade transits Hormuz. QatarEnergy’s urea plant is offline. Urea up 30% in a week. Spring planting is happening now. But sure, they’ll just mine it.

You don’t mine nitrogen fertilizer. The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia, which becomes urea. It’s an industrial chemical process that requires massive quantities of natural gas as feedstock. The gas comes from Qatar (shut down), the Gulf states (can’t ship through Hormuz), and is processed at plants in the Middle East (several offline). You can’t just dig urea out of the ground with a pickaxe.
Phosphate and potash are mined, yes; but phosphate requires sulphur to process, and 45% of global sulphur exports transit Hormuz. So even the stuff you can mine still needs inputs that are stuck in the Persian Gulf right now.

61963095
>>61962539
Manure is great for a backyard garden. Global agriculture runs on synthetic nitrogen produced via the Haber-Bosch process, which requires natural gas as feedstock. There is no substitute at scale. Half of the world’s food production depends on synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, full stop. You cannot replace industrial urea with cow shit for 8 billion people. And while there are different types of fertilizer, they’re all getting hit simultaneously: urea up 30%, ammonia up 16%, phosphate up 6%, sulphur up 7%. Sulphur is needed to MAKE phosphate fertilizer and 45% of global sulphur exports transit Hormuz. You can’t just ‘switch’ when the entire input chain is disrupted at the same chokepoint

>>61962628
Yup. If farmers are getting 30% less nitrogen than they planned to apply, yields drop. Not to zero, but meaningfully. A 10-15% yield reduction across the US Corn Belt ripples through global grain markets for months. And the window to apply is right now, not when the war ends, not when ships start moving again. Every week of rationing is baked into the fall harvest numbers permanently

>>61962952
Fair point and partially true. A lot of fall/winter pre-buying happened. But spring top-dress applications of nitrogen are a separate purchase cycle that’s happening right now, and that’s the part getting disrupted. Also consider that US farmers aren’t the only ones planting. India buys 40%+ of its urea from the Middle East. Three Indian plants have already cut urea output because Qatar LNG stopped flowing. Brazil’s diesel costs are spiking which hits planting economics. This is a global supply chain, not just Iowa. You’re right that the full impact takes time to show up, that’s why this is a weeks to months trade not a days trade

61963105
>>61962954
Ukraine was a direct removal of wheat exports from the market. Prices spiked immediately because traders could quantify exactly how many million tons were gone. This is an input shock, it’s upstream of the wheat itself. The market hasn’t fully priced it because the crop damage hasn’t happened yet, it shows up in September when yields come in lower than expected. That’s the opportunity. By the time it’s obvious to everyone it’ll already be priced in

>>61962454
WEAT holds three CBOT (Chicago Board of Trade) wheat futures contracts spread across different expiration months. Those are Chicago Soft Red Winter wheat contracts, which are US-listed. But here’s the key thing — wheat is a globally fungible commodity. CBOT wheat is the most liquid wheat futures market in the world and its price reflects global supply and demand, not just American conditions.
If India’s crop suffers because three of their urea plants went offline when Qatar LNG stopped flowing, that tightens global wheat supply, which pulls CBOT prices higher because importers compete for available supply from whoever has it. If European farmers under-apply nitrogen because Middle Eastern fertilizer shipments are delayed, that reduces EU production, which means countries that normally buy from Europe bid on US wheat instead, which lifts CBOT.
The global interconnection is actually what makes the thesis so powerful. You’re not betting on one country’s crop having a bad year. You’re betting on a synchronized global fertilizer shock hitting every major growing region simultaneously during the same planting window. India, Brazil, Europe, the US Corn Belt, Southeast Asia — they all depend on fertilizer inputs that transit Hormuz or are produced with gas that transits Hormuz.
